title: Visualizing Embedding Spaces
concept: Using t-SNE or PCA to project 300-dimensional embeddings onto a 2D map reveals semantic clusters of related concepts.

exercises.prompt: How many dimensions do typical high-quality word embeddings have before we compress them?
exercises.explain: Word embeddings typically have hundreds of dimensions (often around 300) to capture complex semantic relationships.

cards.title: Mapping the Invisible
cards.body: How do you visualize a space with 300 dimensions? You can't draw 300 directions on a flat piece of paper! But using clever math, we can squish this high-dimensional space down to a simple 2D map to see what an AI is actually thinking.

cards.title: Dimensionality Reduction
cards.body: Embedding spaces are complex, often containing 300 or more dimensions! Because humans can only see in 3D, we use mathematical tools like t-SNE or PCA. These tools compress those hundreds of dimensions down to a 2D map, showing us how words cluster together based on meaning.

cards.title: The Spice Rack Map
cards.body: Imagine you have a massive box of 300 different spices. It's impossible to arrange them in a straight line by flavor. Instead, you group them on a 2D kitchen counter: sweet spices in one corner, spicy ones in another, and earthy ones in the middle. Dimensionality reduction does the exact same thing with word meanings!

cards.title: Seeing Clusters in Action
cards.body: If you map word embeddings using t-SNE, you will see 'apple', 'banana', and 'cherry' clumped together in a 'fruit' cluster. Far away, you will see another cluster containing 'car', 'train', and 'bicycle' in a 'vehicles' cluster. The map visually proves the AI understands real-world relationships.

cards.title: Myth: Flattening Ruins the Data
cards.body: People often think that compressing 300 dimensions into 2D destroys all useful information. In reality, modern tools like t-SNE are incredibly smart at preserving local relationships, ensuring that words that were close neighbors in 300D remain close neighbors on your 2D screen.

cards.title: From 300D to 2D
cards.body: See how changing the dimensions changes how we view word relationships.

left.text: Unimaginable mathematical space where words are placed by 300 different features.

right.text: A flat, readable map where similar words form clear, visible clusters.

exercises.prompt: Why do we need to compress embeddings to 2D or 3D to look at them?

options.text: Embeddings are secret and not allowed to be looked at.
Computers run out of battery if they show 300 dimensions.

exercises.explain: Humans cannot visualize 300 independent directions (dimensions) at once. We must compress them to 2D or 3D to see them.

exercises.prompt: True or False: We can use tools like t-SNE or PCA to convert 300-dimensional embeddings into a visual 2D map.
exercises.explain: t-SNE and PCA are popular dimensionality reduction techniques specifically used to project high-dimensional data down to 2D or 3D.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how related words appear on an embedding map:
exercises.sentence: When we project high-dimensional embeddings to 2D, related words naturally group together into visual ________.
exercises.explain: Because similar words have similar vectors, compressing them onto a 2D map groups them into visual clusters.

exercises.prompt: Which AI model is demonstrating a POOR understanding of semantic embedding spaces on a 2D map?

options.text: Model C: Clusters 'cat', 'cot', and 'cut' together because they look similar.

exercises.explain: AI Model C is incorrect because standard word embeddings represent meanings, not spelling. 'Cat' and 'Cot' are spelled similarly but have very different meanings, so they shouldn't cluster together.

exercises.prompt: How does a visualization tool like t-SNE help us understand 300-dimensional embeddings?

options.text: It increases the dimensions to 1000 to make the data more complex.
It deletes all words that are not nouns or verbs.
It compresses 300 dimensions into 2D space while keeping related words close together.
It translates words into different languages automatically.

exercises.explain: t-SNE and PCA compress high-dimensional word vectors (like 300 dimensions) down to a simple 2D map, allowing us to physically see clusters of related concepts.
